one_line_takeaway: A robotics/HRI paper — not an AI-security paper — proposing a provably convergent method for a robot to learn an unknown safety constraint in its MPC policy from only the direction (not magnitude) of online human corrections, validated with formal convergence/misspecification guarantees plus simulation, user-study, and real Franka-arm experiments.
key_finding: On the pendulum task, the learned parameter error converged below 0.02 within 17 corrections across 10 runs, matching the paper's own theoretical bound of ln(τ_r ρ_H^r / Vol(Θ0)) / ln(1 - 1/r) = 17.28 (Eq. 30). Baseline comparisons (Table I, pendulum task, 50 independent runs): the proposed method converged in 11.76 ± 2.14 corrections versus 240.38 ± 120.70 corrections for a 'Gradient Matching' baseline (adapted from [57], using raw correction magnitude as a gradient label) — roughly a 20x reduction in required corrections. Against a second 'Maximum Likelihood' baseline (adapted from [58], N=15 correction-trajectory pairs, α=5), the proposed method converged to the true θ_H regardless of the correction-triggering margin ϵ_g (varied 0.1-0.9), while the baseline failed to learn correct weights when ϵ_g was large. An ablation showed MVE-centering vs. random sampling for choosing θi gave similar mean correction counts (11.76 ± 2.14 vs. 11.88 ± 3.50) but random sampling had higher variance and ~25% more compute time. In the quadrotor task, polynomial features required 45 corrections to complete navigation versus only 11 corrections for pre-trained neural SDF features (attributed to the neural features already encoding partial spatial structure); a separate velocity-constraint extension converged within 12 corrections to keep speed under a 0.45 m/s cap. User studies (Table II, N=15 participants, 10 trials each): drone navigation achieved 89.3% ± 7.7% success rate with 15.7 ± 10.6 corrections per successful trial; Franka-arm reaching achieved 89.3% ± 11.2% success with 16.5 ± 6.1 corrections per successful trial. The real-world Franka water-pouring experiment achieved an 84.0% ± 16.7% success rate with 6.88 ± 3.26 corrections per successful trial (5 trials/participant), a slightly lower success rate but fewer corrections than the simulated Franka task, which the authors attribute to participants' unfamiliarity with hardware and to better real-world state perception, respectively.
core_contribution: The paper proposes 'Safe MPC Alignment,' an online method for a robot running a Model Predictive Control (MPC) policy to learn an unknown safety constraint from a human supervisor's real-time directional corrections (only the sign/unit-vector direction of the correction is used, not its magnitude). The safety constraint is parameterized as a linear combination gθ(ξ) = ϕ0(ξ) + θ1ϕ1(ξ) + ... + θrϕr(ξ) over a priori-given features. Each human correction defines a 'cutting set' (a half-space in θ-space, derived from a penalty-barrier reformulation of the safe MPC objective) that intersects with a maintained polytopic hypothesis space Θi. At each round the robot proposes θi as the center of the Maximum Volume inscribed Ellipsoid (MVE) of Θi-1, which the authors prove gives a per-round hypothesis-volume contraction ratio ≤ 1 - 1/r (Lemma 5), yielding (a) a finite-iteration convergence guarantee (Theorem 1, with an explicit bound K on the number of corrections) and (b) an automatic certification procedure for hypothesis misspecification when the true constraint lies outside the initial hypothesis box (Theorem 2). The authors state this is, to their knowledge, the first method for interactively learning safety constraints (as opposed to rewards/costs) from human feedback.
threat_model: Not applicable — this is a robotics/human-robot-interaction control paper, not a security paper. There is no adversary, attacker, or security threat model. 'Safety' here means physical/task safety of a robot's motion (e.g., avoiding collisions, not spilling water), not information security, adversarial robustness, or AI misuse. The closest analog to an adversarial concern the paper itself discusses is human error/irrationality (a well-meaning but mistaken directional correction from the human supervisor degrading the learned constraint), which the paper frames as a robustness limitation, not a threat model.
limitations: The authors' own Discussion section (IX-A) identifies three limitations: (1) Learned constraints are task-specific rather than task-independent — e.g., in the drone-gate task, because targets were placed above the gate, corrections only shaped the top edge of the learned constraint boundary, leaving the bottom edge unconstrained and causing failure on a hypothetical below-gate target; the suggested fix is more diverse task sampling. (2) Robustness against human mistakes is limited — a single erroneous directional correction can produce a wrong hypothesis-space cut (illustrated with a documented failure case, Fig. 23, in the drone game); the authors propose (but do not implement/test) two mitigations: a relaxation slack ϵ in the cutting inequality, and a voting-based selection mechanism. (3) The weight-feature parameterization gθ(ξ) requires the feature vector ϕ(ξ) to be specified a priori (manually or via a pre-trained network); poor feature selection can prevent the method from representing the true intended constraint, and feature selection itself is explicitly left out of scope. Additionally (not separately flagged by authors but evident from the results text), all quantitative baseline comparisons and convergence analyses use synthetic/simulated corrections rather than real human input, and the real-world evaluation was limited to a single task (water pouring) with one robot.

datasets_used: No external datasets; all data is self-generated via simulation (inverted pendulum dynamics; MuJoCo quadrotor zigzag-tube environment; MuJoCo drone-navigation-through-gate game; MuJoCo Franka-arm reaching-through-slot game) or a real-world Franka Emika robot arm mobile water-pouring setup with N=15 recruited ASU human participants (13 male, 2 female) under IRB approval (STUDY00020060)
quantitative_results: Pendulum: parameter error < 0.02 within 17 corrections (10 runs), matching theoretical bound K=17.28
Pendulum baseline comparison (Table I, 50 runs): Safe MPC Alignment 11.76 ± 2.14 corrections vs. Gradient Matching baseline 240.38 ± 120.70 corrections
Ablation: MVE centering 11.76 ± 2.14 corrections vs. random sampling 11.88 ± 3.50 corrections (random sampling ~25% slower computationally)
Quadrotor: polynomial features required 45 corrections vs. neural SDF features required 11 corrections to complete navigation; velocity-cap constraint (0.45 m/s) learned within 12 corrections

User study (Table II, N=15, 10 trials each): drone navigation 89.3% ± 7.7% success rate, 15.7 ± 10.6 corrections/success; Franka arm reaching 89.3% ± 11.2% success rate, 16.5 ± 6.1 corrections/success
Real-world Franka water pouring: 84.0% ± 16.7% success rate, 6.88 ± 3.26 corrections per successful trial (5 trials/participant, success = >50g water poured)
baselines_compared: Gradient Matching baseline (adapted from cited work [57]): uses raw human correction vector directly as gradient label, loss L(θ) = ||a + ∇B(ξ,θ)||^2

Maximum Likelihood baseline (adapted from cited work [58]): treats e^{gθ(ξ)} as probability of a 'stop-feedback', trained via maximum likelihood with a softplus regularizer on N=15 trajectory-correction pairs, α=5
Random sampling of θi from the hypothesis polytope (ablation against the paper's own MVE-centering choice)
